segment-text: Screenplay by Ron Nyswaner
4000 Warner Blvd. Burbank, CA 91522
EXT. PEACEHAVEN UK - DAY - 1999

TOM BURGESS (60s) walks his dog along rocky bluffs edging the sea. It's a blustery day. Tom has the build of a lifelong athlete, though his face has become rugged with sun exposure. His broad shoulders are hunched with tension. PAST TOM -- Plain houses of working people and pensioners.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM - DAY - 1999

MARION BURGESS (60s, retired school teacher) empties a dresser, transferring her husband's clothes into a box. BEHIND MARION -- A hospital bed with crisp linens waits for a house guest. On the dresser a vase of violets. She notes a wedding PHOTO on the wall: Marion and Tom, forty years earlier. She reaches for the photo, to take it down. But changes her mind, leaving it in place.
INT./EXT. AMBULANCE/PEACEHAVEN HIGH STREET - DAY - 1999
An AMBULANCE rumbles along the main route through town. PATRICK HAZELWOOD (70s), strapped into a wheelchair, stares out the window at local shops: Joe's Pizza, Kebab House.
INT./EXT. AMBULANCE/BRAMBER AVENUE - DAY - 1999
Patrick's jaw hangs limp (from a stroke). But the disdain he feels for the area oozes from still lively eyes.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM/HALLWAY - DAY - 1999

Marion continues to move Tom's personal items. She's what people call a "handsome" woman. Her clothes are tasteful without attracting attention. Educated middle class. She hears activity outside. Goes to the window. SEES -- The AMBULANCE arriving. Suddenly anxious, she moves quickly to greet the ambulance and BUMPS her thigh against the corner of the dresser. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.2.
EXT. BURGESS HOUSE - DAY - 1999
Marion steps out as a Nurse and Medical Aide unload Patrick. She starts down the pavement to assist. Shall I -- ?
NIGEL (32, Nigerian) wheels Patrick toward the house.
Best if you let us do it. Right.
Marion steps out of the way. And SEES -- Tom. Keeping his distance.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM - MINUTES LATER - 1999
Marion observes from the doorway as Nigel and PAMELA (20's, medical aide) transfer Patrick to the bed.
There you go, Mr. Hazelwood. You've landed in quite a lovely spot, haven't you? And with a view of the sea. (to Marion) Are you a nurse?
Teacher. I mean, I was. I'm retired.
(laughs, to Patrick) After a week with you, she'll wish she was back in the classroom, won't she?
Patrick's eyes meet Marion's.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - LIVING/DINING AREA - MINUTES LATER 1999
Nigel unloads intimidating medical supplies onto the table: pills, ointments, rubber gloves, special tableware, etc. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.3.

Pamela will be here every day, to bathe and exercise him. I'll come round twice a week to check his vitals and adjust his medication. But you'll have charge of his meals and anything that comes up when we're not here.
I understand.

He can feed himself, though he sometimes makes a mess of it. And you have to watch for choking from the dysphagia. That's -MARION Difficulty in swallowing, I know. I'm to cut his food into small pieces, or puree it. And make sure he drinks a sufficient amount of water to prevent dehydration. I've been reading up.
Well you deserve a gold star, don't you? (reaching for something)
And this -He places a cardboard box onto the table.
Mr. Hazelwood's personal items. They came to the hospital after you sold the house. I didn't.
Your family, I mean.
No, I'm not -(dropping the subject) Is there anything else I should know?
Talk to him. Get him to answer if you can. Speech is the last thing to come back.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.4.
You'll be fine. Patients do better in a home setting. He might come along quicker than you expect. I'm off then.
And no cigarettes! He'll try to get one out of you.
We don't keep them in the house.
EXT. BEACH - DAY - 1999
Tom is walking. Hands in pockets, the dog trailing.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM - MINUTES LATER - 1999 10
Patrick takes in the room's decor with a critical eye: second hand dresser, faded art print, etc. His eyes land on the wedding photo. It irks him. Marion enters. With forced cheerfulness.
All settled in? I hope you won't be bored. I can put a telly in here, if you like. (off his look) Right. You were never fond of television. I know. I'll go to the library, to fetch you some books.
Patrick raises his bent, disabled hands.
Oh. You'd have trouble holding a book, wouldn't you?
He is stone-faced. Not giving her anything.
Patrick. I know this must be rather awkward for you. You were always so independent. But I'm going to do my best to make you comfortable. I hope you will, at least, meet me halfway.
His expression softens. A little.
I better see to lunch.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.5. INT. BURGESS HOUSE - DINING AREA - HALF HOUR LATER - 1999 11
Marion sets the table for three. Examining the modified tableware as she places it at the third setting.
(looking at spoon or fork) Just put both.
Tom enters with Walter (the dog).
Here you go, boy! Have a treat. Good boy!
He notices three place settings on the table.
What's this about?
The literature says you should feed a stroke patient at the family table, so they don't feel isolated. You'll have to help me get him into
his wheelchair.
I thought he was staying in the spare room.
He's not a prisoner.
This is my home, isn't it? For God's sake, Marion, I can only stand so much.
He won't take a step towards the table until she takes away the third setting. Stand-off. Marion removes the plate, etc. Tom takes a seat, helping himself to cold meat.
There are places for invalids.
Marion begins making a tray for Patrick's lunch.
Have you been inside any of those places? Besides, you agreed.
You wouldn't give me any rest until I did.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.6.
It seemed the right thing to do.
Don't fool yourself. You know why you brought him here. Why?
To punish him.
That's ridiculous.
Marion returns to arranging Patrick's tray.
You waiting on him hand and foot. That's ridiculous.
EXT. PEACEHAVEN - PUB - NIGHT - 1999
Quiet streets. Closed shops. Only signs of life come from a pub.
EXT. PEACEHAVEN - STREETS - NIGHT - 1999 EXT. BURGESS HOUSE - NIGHT - 1999 INT. BURGESS HOUSE - MASTER BEDROOM - NIGHT - 1999
Marion is awake, alone in bed. She rises. Puts on slippers and flannel dressing gown.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - LIVING/DINING AREA - CONTINUOUS - 199914

Marion finds Tom in the living room, asleep on the sofa. She covers him with a blanket. Reaches to pull off his slippers. But he struggles against her touch. She steps back. She moves down the corridor. Pauses outside the spare room. STUDIES -- Patrick, asleep. Dimly lit by a street lamp. His face retains its elegance. He still has presence.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - MASTER BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS - 1999
Marion returns to the master bedroom. Now, her room. She's not going to be able to sleep.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.7.

The cardboard box holding Patrick's possessions rests on the dresser. She can't resist temptation. She unpacks the box finding paltry treasures from a long life: tortoise shell comb, mirror, shoe horn, cuff links. A dog-eared copy of Anna Karenina. What she finds next: three red, leather-bound journals, embossed with gold letters: Patrick Hazelwood. She opens one of the journals to the first page: "March 14, 1957," and a long entry in Patrick's elegant hand. She snaps the journal shut. Is it because she's invading his privacy? Or she's afraid of what she will find?
EXT. BURGESS HOUSE - MORNING - 1999
Tom exits the house, bundled against the cold. Walter the dog trots beside him.
Come on, boy!
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE ROOM - MORNING - 1999
Patrick's possessions have been laid out on the dresser: cuff links, comb, mirror, etc. But not the journals. Marion supervises Patrick's breakfast. He's feeding himself porridge and struggling.
(reaching) May I...?
Patrick, resisting assistance, jerks the spoon out of Marion's reach -- splattering porridge over the wall. He croaks out a laugh.
I can I try and help you eat...
He shakes his head and utters a strangled negative -Ngh.
-- and goes back to digging at the porridge.
Fine. Suit yourself.
She starts wiping porridge off the wallpaper. They ignore each other until...
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.8.

Patrick drops his spoon. Not far from Marion's feet. An accident. He waits for Marion to pick it up. But Marion, completely aware of the spoon resting on the floor, continues wiping the wall. He gestures to the spoon with his disabled hand. Agh....
She ignores him. Until -TOM

Walter! Catch it, boy! Patrick twists toward the window and Tom's voice, pulling on the sheet where the breakfast tray is resting... The tray SLIDES off the bed and CRASHES to the floor. Marion whips around, startled. Patrick looks sheepish.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - KITCHEN - DUSK - 1999
Marion tosses pieces of the broken bowl into the bin.
I don't...I don't know what I am...
Tom enters. Notices the mess.
What happened?
She doesn't answer. Tom observes her for a moment. Then turns to the dog.
Here, boy. Treat!
Now -- Marion watches Tom as he offers unrestrained affection to their retriever.
EXT. PEACEHAVEN CHALK CLIFFS - ANOTHER DAY - 1999
Waves break near the stark cliffs. PRE-LAP:
Here's an interesting bit in the Arts Section...
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.9. INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM - DAY - 1999
Marion has been reading to Patrick from the local paper. He does not appear much interested.
A young woman's been short-listed for the Turner Prize with a piece called "My Bed." And that's exactly what it is. Unmade no less.
She holds up the paper, showing a photograph of the controversial art piece. Patrick snorts with contempt.
Apparently everyone's talking about it. I'd like to see it. You were the one who taught me to look at art. Do you remember?
He produces half a word.
He brings two trembling fingers to his lips. Ohk.
I don't know what you're -OHK! I --
Patrick shakes his two raised fingers in the air. Sm-ohk!
She gets it.
Smoke? You want a smoke. He nods.
Nigel said you can't. AGH!
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.10.
You've had a stroke. Smoking raises the blood pressure -AGGGGGGGGH!
-- could cause you to have another -He shocks Marion by going into a RAGE -- wildly flinging his
arms, shaking his body.
I'm sorry, Patrick, but I absolutely
cannot allow you to -He gains control of one arm and POINTS to the DOOR.
He nods.

Fine. (turns at the door, anger pouring out) I didn't have to bring you here. I only knew you were in hospital because your nephew wrote to me. He said the rest of your family would have nothing to do with you. And he lives in Sheffield, so could I look in? One of the nurses told me they were sending you to an awful place. And I -- I don't know why -- I said I'd take you in. It was a moment's impulse but I was too embarrassed to take it back. Besides, I thought... I don't know what I thought. Tom was so angry. He still is. I know this is hard for you. But at least you could try to... Appear to... I only brought you here because no one else wanted you!
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - LIVING/DINING AREA - CONT. - 1999
-- And storms into the living room. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.11.
Why did I -- ? Stupid.

She yanks open a drawer, digs into the back and FINDS -A pack of cigarettes. Hidden treasure. (Obviously she lied to Nigel about keeping cigarettes in the house.) She lights one and savors the first drag. Then settles at the window. Where she SEES -Tom. Just his figure in the distance. Heading towards the house from his walk. She keeps her GAZE fixed on him, as he gets closer, and reveals himself to be -Tom from 42 years ago. And we CUT TO -22
EXT. BRIGHTON BEACH - DAY - 1957

YOUNG TOM (24, dressed very much like his older self in working man's clothes) WALKS toward camera. Smiles at -MARION (22). In a modest bathing suit and terrycloth beach "coverup," standing next to Tom's sister, SYLVIE (22).
You said you'd ask.
It was your idea.
He's your brother.
Marion gazes admiringly at Tom -- strong build, playful smile, hearty laugh.
I wouldn't get my hopes up for Tom. I'm not --
He likes the loud, busty type.
Tom and Roy bound over. Marion can't help but notice how Tom's shirt sticks to his frame. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.12.
What do you think, ladies? Are we getting wet today?
Marion can't swim.
I can swim. Just not very well.
She wants a lesson, Tom, but she's too shy to ask. Sylvie!
(reaching for Sylvie) How 'bout I give you a lesson?
Roy chases Sylvie down the beach, their laughter ECHOING back to Tom and Marion. Awkward now they're alone.
They're a couple of cards, aren't they?
Marion does not take her eyes off Tom.
Almost didn't recognise you. It's been a while since you came 'round to the house.
I've been in Teacher Training College.
Sylvie said.
And you were stationed -TOM Portsmouth. Glad to have that behind me. I'm a policeman now.
I heard. That must be quite fulfilling.
The conversation dies. Neither is adept at it. Until -- WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.13.
You can't go through life afraid of the water.
EXT. BRIGHTON BEACH - IN THE WATER - DAY - 1957
Tom leads Marion into the waves, guiding her by the elbow, until they're waist deep.
When I give the word, throw your feet behind.
It's cold.
You'll get used to it.
He positions himself at her side.
Marion flattens to the water and throws her feet behind. Ow!
Tom reaches for his just-kicked groin.
I'm so sorry!
EXT. BRIGHTON BEACH - IN THE WATER - LATER - 1957

Marion glides through the water, trying a breast stroke, while Tom walks at her side, holding her up. MARION'S POV GLANCING BEHIND -- Tom, head and shoulders above the water. Steady and calm. Taking the lesson seriously. TOM'S HAND -- On her waist. MARION -- Closes her eyes as a wave washes over her.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - LIVING/DINING AREA - DAY - 1999
Smoke curls around Marion as she remembers.
EXT. BRIGHTON BEACH - DAY - 1957
Marion and Tom leave the beach in street clothes. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.14.
Thanks for the lesson.
She offers her hand. He shakes it.
You did quite well. You should stick with it.
Thanks. I suppose I'll head home. Although she doesn't want to leave, there's not much else to
do. She turns to go...
I was thinking...
She turns back eagerly.
You being a teacher, you must be a bit of a bookworm.
(laughs) Well, I don't know how to take that. But, I suppose it's true.
Oh no, I didn't mean... You see, I'm not much of a reader. Oh.
But I believe a man ought to try to improve himself.
I agree with you there.
Do you think you could recommend some books to me? In exchange, I could give you more swimming lessons.
Well, I would -TOM You must be busy. It's not long before school starts.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.15.
No! I mean. Yes. I'd be happy to. Recommend books. And more lessons, if you're willing. Smashing.
INT. BRIGHTON LIBRARY - DAY - 1957
Marion leads Tom through tall shelves. He looks around, as if he's never been there. Because he hasn't.
Do you have any particular subject in mind? Literature, history, or...?
Anything you suggest.
Let's try it this way. What made you decide to take up reading?
Well... My work. I meet a great many people. And it's my responsibility to converse with them intelligently.
That doesn't really narrow it down.
Why do you like to read?
Well. When I read a good book, I lose myself in whatever world I'm reading about. I forget who I am and all my little insecurities. I'm sure you don't have that problem.
What problem?
Insecurity. About anything.
Don't judge a book by its cover.
She laughs. He's proud of his witty response. shushed by a MALE LIBRARIAN.
(lowers his voice) What about art?
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.16.
You mean, books about art?
I never really looked at art before. But now, I think I could get something out of it.
That's a start. Do you have a particular artist in mind?
Is there a painter named Turner?
(hiding a smile) That would be J.M.W. Turner. I think the art section's this way.
Marion heads down an aisle. Tom, about to follow, finds the Male Librarian ogling him.
INT. LIBRARY - PRIVATE STALL - DAY - 1957
Tom and Marion share a quiet corner, pouring over books. Tom studies "Art Through the Ages" with intensity. Marion peers over the top of her book -- to study Tom.
EXT. BRIGHTON BEACH - IN THE WATER - DAY - 1957

Tom swims next to Marion. Her skills have improved. But she can't resist taking sideways glances at Tom as his strong arms cut through the surf. He thoroughly enjoys himself in the water, free of self-consciousness. She's in shallow water and so she stands, while Tom keeps swimming, his back catching the sun. And she watches.
EXT. BRIGHTON BOARDWALK - DAY - 1957
Marion and Tom walk past the food stalls and tattoo parlours fronting the beach. They have been swimming.
My first week at school may not have been a brilliant success, but I think I'm going to be a good teacher.
There's no doubt. I read two entire books this week. On duty, when it was slow.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.17.
Marion puts her arm through his. He notices.
I'm proud of you.
And I can say the same. You've become an excellent swimmer.
We are not finished with the lessons, are we?
The water was freezing today. Didn't you notice? No.
Well, you hardly need any more lessons, you've got so much better. I should give you a certificate, like they do in the swimming club. Or a trophy.
You still can.
Tom's puzzled. Until he follows Marion's look to -- the Shooting Gallery arcade stall.
INT. BRIGHTON PIER - ARCADE - MINUTES LATER - 1957

DING! DING! FLASHING LIGHTS! Tom fires off one, two shots (getting closer to the bullseye) then HITTING IT with his final shot. He raises one arm like a champion and throws the other around Marion. Plants a quick kiss on her cheek. The Arcade Barker hands over a prize. Tom accepts congratulations from Bystanders. This simple triumph gives him great pleasure. MARION'S CLOSE POV OF TOM'S FACE -- So happy, as he turns into the sun. As he turns back TOWARD CAMERA WE SEE -32
EXT. BURGESS HOUSE - ANOTHER DAY - DUSK - 1999
Tom (in his 60's) as he steps outside, facing the sun. Going for a walk. Walter bounds out with him. But Tom orders the dog back inside.
Go on, boy. Get in there. You're staying home.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.18.
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - KITCHEN - DUSK - 1999
Marion finds Walter near the door, looking dejected.
What's wrong, Walter? Did Tom leave you behind today?
INT. BURGESS HOUSE - SPARE BEDROOM - MOMENTS LATER - DUSK -34
Marion enters, with Patrick's tray.
Here's a cup of tea.
She sets down the tray. And the pack of cigarettes. Patrick looks up surprised.
Shall I light one for you?
(almost normal sounding) Pu-please. Good.
She places a cigarette into his mouth.
Tom stopped two years ago and there's nothing worse than a reformed smoker.
She lights the cigarette for him.
I keep these hidden in the back of the drawer.
Patrick holds the cigarette with shaking fingers. Inhales deeply. With immense satisfaction.
Mind if I join you?
He shrugs. She lights one for herself.
Tom's out for one of his walks. I don't mind being alone. Not too much. You get used to it. Although... (should she confess this?) It's embarrassing.
But sometimes I talk to myself. Little things. On and off through the day. I catch myself and wonder if I'm going batty.
Patrick nods, commiserating.
They say if you think you might be going crazy, you aren't. So, we can hold onto that. Ye-yes.
I better get on with your supper.
As she collects the tray, her LOOK drifts to the WEDDING PHOTO on the wall, hers and Tom's youthful, happy faces.
EXT. BRIGHTON - WORKING CLASS NEIGHBORHOOD - DAY - 1957
A car -- decorated with paper flowers and a "Just Married" sign -- is parked in the street.
INT. TOM'S PARENTS' HOUSE - DAY - 1957 An at-home wedding party for Sylvie (Tom's sister) and Roy

(Tom's pal). Roy is half-drunk, necktie askew. Mother of the bride, MRS. BURGESS (50's, weary), entertains neighbours and relatives. GORDON BURGESS (50's), Tom and Sylvie's dad, a belligerent alcoholic, scowls at everyone. Marion delivers a plate of sausage rolls to Tom.
Thank you. You look nice, by the way. Did I tell you?
Not yet. But thank you just the same.
ACROSS THE ROOM -- Sylvie attempts to pry a pint of whiskey from Roy's hand.
Leave me be, will ya?
You've had enough.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.20.
Looks as if Sylvie and Roy are about to have their first row as a married couple.
Won't be their last.
Tom's father -- drunk -- addresses the party.
As the father of the bride, I just want to say -- Today, I didn't lose a daughter, I got another mouth to feed. Dad!
Not my fault I can't find a job. I'm trying.
Trying to sponge off your betters you mean.
Lay off him, Dad.
Lay off him, says my son, the copper. You'd been better staying in the kitchen. That's what the navy trained you for, ain't it? Didn't even learn how to fire a rifle.
I can shoot just fine.
I can attest to that.
And now he's a bobby. Dishing out traffic tickets and the like.
I'm a police constable. And a promotion to Sergeant could well be on the cards at some point.
I got no time for bloody coppers. Always sticking their nose in.
Mr. Burgess stumbles over an ottoman and crashes to the floor. Sylvie runs up the stairs, crying. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.21.
I better go see to her.
Tom crosses to help his Dad.
Take your hands off me, bloody copper!
EXT. TOM'S PARENTS' HOUSE - LATER - 1957
Tom smokes. Watching boys playing football in the street. Marion finds him.
There you are.
How's Sylvie?
Marriage isn't exactly what she thought it would be. How's your Dad?
Out cold in his bed. He managed to ruin Sylvie's wedding.
It's not the wedding that matters. It's whether two people care about each other. If they make good companions.
That's not the way most girls talk.
How do they talk?
You know. Romantic. And that's what most of 'em want to hear. Only I've never been good at it.
Maybe it's possible to be romantic and good friends.
Tom studies Marion.
You're quite an unusual girl.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.22.
Thanks. That was a compliment, wasn't it?
Course it was.
He throws his cigarette into the street.
Are you free Saturday?
I'll have to check my diary. Oh.
I'm joking. Yes, I'm free. What do you have in mind? She's hoping: Dinner? Dancing? A movie?
I'd like to surprise you.
EXT. BRIGHTON ART MUSEUM - DAY - 1957
Marion (in skirt and heels) and Tom (in jacket and tie) approach the museum. Her hand tucked through his arm. She is surprised by the venue for their date. The museum.
I'm sure you've been before. Well, yes.
But have you ever had a personal tour from the Director of the Western Art Galleries?
No. How did you...?
He was the witness to an accident on my watch. Nothing serious. But we got to talking. And he invited me for a private tour and I thought, Marion would enjoy that sort of thing more than me --
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.23.
They sweep through the entrance doors -39 INT./EXT. BRIGHTON ART MUSEUM - ENTRANCE HALL - CONT. - 1957
And he invited you to join.
There you are.
APPROACHING -- PATRICK HAZELWOOD -- In his 30's. Elegant. Tailored suit. Extends his hand to Tom.
So glad you've come.
Patrick Hazelwood. This is Marion Taylor.
Patrick warmly grasps Marion's hand.
Delighted. Tom says you've turned him into a scholar.
He's an eager student.
With a teacher as attractive as you, who wouldn't be? Shall we have a tour?
INT. ART MUSEUM - PAINTING GALLERY - DAY - 1957
Patrick analyses a Turner seascape. Flanked by Tom and Marion.
Notice the light striking the crest of the crashing waves. You feel they could crush you or take you under.
INT. ART MUSEUM - PAINTING GALLERY - DAY - 1957
Patrick traces a line in the air, above the dancers in William Blake's Oberon, Titania, and Puck.
Blake's trying to startle the senses
much passion in his work. WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.24. Marion finds herself inspired by Patrick's enthusiasm. Or is it his handsome profile and manicured hand?
You have to let it take hold of you.
INT. ART MUSEUM - PAINTING GALLERY - DAY - 1957

Patrick, Marion, and Tom stand in silence in front of the stunning Raising of Lazarus by Jan Lievens. Patrick doesn't move. Mesmerised. This is his favourite painting. He slowly exhales at its power. Tom fidgets, unsure what they're meant to be doing. But Marion tries to "dive" into the painting. AS MARION GAZES AT THE PAINTING -- She imagines she hears the startled cries of Lazarus's relatives as he reaches out of the grave at the command of an illumined Christ. Astonishing.
Patrick smiles at her. Exactly.
INT. BRIGHTON ART MUSEUM - DAY - 1957
Patrick escorts them to the door.
Your students are quite lucky to have a beautiful young teacher like you. I hope you'll bring them for a visit.
I'll try to arrange it. I've been to the museum before, but I never really appreciated what we have here.
Tom thrusts his hand into Patrick's, clearly proud of having arranged a successful outing.
Thanks ever so much. It's been splendid.
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.25.
I don't know if you're free Friday, but I've got tickets to a recital. Why don't the two of you join me?
Friday's the night we usually go to the library.
Marion puts her arm through Tom's.
He meets me after class.
Funny, though, I've never been to a recital. But it's up to Marion. She's on the spot. Of course.
See you then! Seven o'clock.
EXT. PAVILLION GARDENS - MINUTES LATER - 1957
Tom and Marion walk through the gardens. Marion feels uneasy but isn't quite sure why.
That's so kind of him to invite us. But I'll be sorry to miss our library visit.
Don't you want to go? I could tell
No. It would hurt his feelings. He must not have many friends his own
age, if he's asked us.
He has plenty. I think he's taken with you, that's all.
Don't be silly!
You started all this. What?
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.26.
My self-improvement. And now Patrick's helping me too. Helping you?

To improve my mind. (seriously) I want to be a better man than my father. I want to prove something to him. To everyone. But if you don't like Patrick, it means nothing to me. I'll tell him we can't go.
But I do like him, Tom. I do.
INT. CONCERT HALL - NIGHT - 1957
Patrick, Marion, and Tom occupy three seats in the opulent,
old hall, listening to a recital.
Marion catches Tom trying to keep his eyes open. She glances at Patrick, caught up in the music. Their eyes meet, making a connection over their shared pleasure.
INT. BRIGHTON CAFE - NIGHT - 1957
Marion, Tom, and Patrick, in a cafe, on their second bottle of wine, with Patrick refilling their glasses.
I love this wine. It reminds me of
Have you traveled a great deal?
The museum sends me to Italy now and then, to collect new pieces. And I always take a few days for myself, to have an adventure. How about you?
I've never had the opportunity to travel. And I don't know when I will.
But you must dream, Marion! Nothing happens if you don't dream of it first. Isn't that right, Tom?
WHITE SHOOTING 4-6-21 p.27.
(buzzed from the wine) Dream, Marion! Go on.
What's your fantasy destination? Paris? Milan? The Greek Isles?
I suppose it would be... Venice.
You're a romantic!
I imagine Venice is like something, I don't know, from another world.
That it is. And I can see you strolling across the Piazza San Marco, all the Venetian ragazzi admiring your beautiful legs. And you, Tom?
I'm happy at home. There can't be any place nicer than Brighton.
That kind of contentment is a noble trait.
But, Tom, you must want to travel somewhere.
Why should I?
Because spending your entire life in one place is just dull.
Tom is offended.
Then I suppose I'm dull. My apologies. I didn't --
I like Brighton and I'll not apologise for it.
